meta.title: Fraternidade Sacerdotal São Pio X — Cronologia
meta.subtitle: Chronology of the Society of Saint Pius X (1970–present)
meta.description: An open, source-referenced chronology of the Fraternidade Sacerdotal São Pio X (Society of Saint Pius X, SSPX/FSSPX) — the traditionalist Catholic priestly society founded by Archbishop Marcel Lefebvre in 1970 — and its relationship with the Holy See.
meta.dataQualityNote: This is a work-in-progress dataset compiled from public sources (see references). The Society's canonical status is contested and time-sensitive: characterizations are attributed to their authors (the Holy See, the SSPX itself, commentators), never asserted in the site's own voice, and dates not yet verified against a primary source are flagged with a ? mark. Corrections against primary sources are welcome.

facts.label: What it is
facts.value: An international priestly society of the traditionalist Catholic movement, dedicated to the formation of priests and the preservation of the traditional Latin Mass (1962 Missal) and pre-conciliar doctrine and practice.

facts.label: Founded
facts.value: 1 November 1970, Fribourg, Switzerland — erected as a pia unio ad experimentum by Bishop François Charrière of Lausanne, Geneva and Fribourg.

facts.label: Founder
facts.value: Archbishop Marcel Lefebvre (1905–1991), former Archbishop of Dakar and Superior General of the Holy Ghost Fathers.

facts.label: Headquarters
facts.value: Menzingen, Switzerland; mother seminary at Écône (Valais).

facts.label: Scale (self-reported, January 2026)
facts.value: 733 priests, 264 seminarians, 5 seminaries (Écône, Dillwyn, Flavigny, Zaitzkofen, La Reja), 760 Mass centers in 77 countries. Lay-adherent estimates range from ~100,000 (independent estimates) to ~600,000 (SSPX's own figure).

facts.label: In Brazil
facts.value: Casa Autônoma do Brasil ('district in formation') since 2017, previously under the Argentina-based District of South America; Brazilian seminarians are formed at La Reja, Argentina.

facts.label: Canonical status
facts.value: Time-sensitive and contested: approved 1970–1975; canonically irregular from 1975, though repeatedly described by Vatican officials as not formally schismatic; since the 2 July 2026 DDF decree, the Holy See describes the Society's clergy as in schism — a decree the SSPX rejects as invalid.

events.title: Founding of the SSPX
events.text: Bishop François Charrière erects the Society as a pia unio on an ad experimentum basis for six years; Marcel Lefebvre is founder and superior.

events.title: Écône seminary opens
events.text: The International Seminary of Saint Pius X receives its first cohort of 24 candidates; 32 more enter in October 1972.

events.title: Canonical visitation
events.text: Two Belgian priests inspect the seminary on behalf of a commission of cardinals.

events.title: Lefebvre's Declaration
events.text: Lefebvre professes adherence to 'eternal Rome' while rejecting 'the Rome of neo-modernist tendencies' — the text that became the Society's ideological charter. The date is verified against the official text published by the SSPX General House, dated 21 November 1974.

events.title: Approval withdrawn
events.text: Bishop Pierre Mamie withdraws the Society's pia unio status with the approval of the Congregation for Religious and Paul VI; the SSPX disputes the legality of the procedure.

events.title: The Palmar consecrations — an Écône personnel link
events.text: Fr. Maurice Revaz, canon of the Grand-Saint-Bernard and canon-law professor at Écône, drives Archbishop Ngô Đình Thục from Italy to El Palmar de Troya (arriving Christmas Eve 1975). On 11 January 1976 Thục consecrates five Palmarian bishops — declared excommunicated by the nuncio to Spain on 15 January — and on 28 January Revaz himself is consecrated a Palmarian bishop by Clemente Domínguez and others, placing him in the Palmarian, not the Thục, lineage; he leaves the movement about two years later (Lundberg). Lefebvre took no part; testimony reported by Lundberg has him declining an invitation to Palmar and pointing the group to Thục — attributed testimony, not established fact.

events.title: The 'hot summer' ordinations
events.text: Lefebvre ordains priests despite an explicit prohibition, after being publicly rebuked by Paul VI in consistory on 24 May.

events.title: Suspension a divinis
events.text: After a 1 July suspension from conferring orders and continued refusal to comply, Lefebvre is suspended from all sacramental ministry.

events.title: Guérard des Lauriers dismissed from Écône
events.text: Fr. Michel-Louis Guérard des Lauriers OP, professor of theology at the seminary from 1971, is dismissed in autumn 1977 after teaching the vacant-see thesis to seminarians in a September retreat despite Lefebvre's prohibition — the SSPX account (Si Si No No, 1998) and his sedeprivationist biographer (IMBC) agree on the dismissal and its ground. Consecrated by Thục in 1981, he became the source of the [[sedeprivationism|sedeprivationist]] ('Cassiciacum') lineages — see the episcopal genealogy.

events.title: Lefebvre meets John Paul II
events.text: Shortly after the papal election, Lefebvre seeks regularization; talks continue intermittently through the 1980s.

events.title: Latin American seminary founded
events.text: The Seminario Nuestra Señora Corredentora is founded in the Buenos Aires priory and moves to La Reja in 1981 — the formation house for Latin America, including Brazil.

events.title: Schmidberger succeeds Lefebvre
events.text: Fr. Franz Schmidberger becomes Superior General (1982–1994); Lefebvre remains the Society's episcopal head.

events.title: The 'Nine' leave — SSPV founded
events.text: Fr. Clarence Kelly and eight US priests are expelled from or leave the SSPX in a dispute over the 1962 Missal and openness to sedevacantism, founding the Society of Saint Pius V — a distinct, sedevacantist-leaning body.

events.title: Consecrations announced
events.text: Lefebvre announces his intention to consecrate bishops without a papal mandate, citing his age and the need to continue ordinations.

events.title: Protocol signed — and withdrawn
events.text: Lefebvre and Cardinal Ratzinger sign a protocol of agreement on 5 May. On 6 May Lefebvre writes to Ratzinger announcing he will proceed with episcopal consecrations by 30 June (letter text published by SSPX Asia); 'Ecclesia Dei' treats his letter of 2 June 1988 as the definitive refusal.

events.title: The Écône consecrations
events.text: Lefebvre, with Bishop Antônio de Castro Mayer (emeritus of Campos, Brazil) as co-consecrator, consecrates Bernard Fellay, Bernard Tissier de Mallerais, Richard Williamson and Alfonso de Galarreta without papal mandate.

events.title: Excommunications declared
events.text: The Congregation for Bishops declares that Lefebvre, Castro Mayer and the four new bishops incurred [[latae-sententiae|latae sententiae]] [[excommunication|excommunication]].

events.title: 'Ecclesia Dei'
events.text: John Paul II's [[motu-proprio|motu proprio]] calls the consecrations 'a schismatic act' and establishes the Ecclesia Dei Commission for faithful attached to the traditional liturgy.

events.title: FSSP founded
events.text: Twelve former SSPX priests led by Fr. Josef Bisig found the Priestly Fraternity of Saint Peter in full communion with Rome.

events.title: Campos priests form the Priestly Union of Saint John Mary Vianney
events.text: The priests loyal to Bishop Castro Mayer formally organize the União Sacerdotal São João Maria Vianney, continuing the traditional Mass across the diocese of Campos.

events.title: Death of Marcel Lefebvre
events.text: The founder dies at 85 and is buried at the Écône seminary.

events.title: Death of Antônio de Castro Mayer
events.text: The co-consecrator of the 1988 Écône consecrations dies a month after Lefebvre. Source conflict recorded: Wikipedia (EN/PT) and the SSPX obituary date the death 25 April 1991; catholic-hierarchy.org gives 26 April 1991.

events.title: Rangel consecrated for the Campos union
events.text: Licínio Rangel is consecrated without papal mandate by Bishop Tissier de Mallerais, assisted by de Galarreta and Williamson, to lead the Campos priests after Castro Mayer's death.

events.title: Fellay elected Superior General
events.text: Bishop Bernard Fellay leads the Society 1994–2018 (re-elected 2006).

events.title: Jubilee pilgrimage to Rome
events.text: Over 5,000 SSPX pilgrims visit the major basilicas; Cardinal Castrillón Hoyos opens contacts, reportedly offering a personal prelature or apostolic administration.

events.title: Campos priests reconcile with Rome
events.text: The priestly union founded by Castro Mayer's followers is received as the Personal Apostolic Administration Saint John Mary Vianney under Bishop Rangel; Fernando Rifan, consecrated in full communion later that year, succeeds him in December — a path the SSPX declined.

events.title: 'Summorum Pontificum'
events.text: Benedict XVI liberalizes the 1962 Missal as the 'extraordinary form', meeting one of the SSPX's two stated preconditions for talks.

events.title: 1988 excommunications remitted
events.text: A decree of the Congregation for Bishops (dated 21 January, published 24 January) lifts the excommunications of the four bishops; some sources cite 10 March 2009, the date of Benedict XVI's explanatory letter.

events.title: Williamson affair
events.text: A television interview in which Bishop Richard Williamson denied the Holocaust airs just before the remission is announced, causing worldwide controversy; the SSPX removes him as rector of the La Reja seminary.

events.title: Doctrinal talks begin
events.text: Discussions between the CDF and the SSPX on Vatican II (religious liberty, ecumenism, collegiality) run until 2012 without producing an agreement.

events.title: Williamson expelled
events.text: The SSPX expels Bishop Williamson after unauthorized confirmations in Brazil and public defiance; he goes on to lead the loose 'Resistance' network.

events.title: Resistance consecration in Brazil
events.text: Williamson consecrates Jean-Michel Faure without mandate at the Santa Cruz Monastery, consolidating the 'Resistance' splinter. The local ordinary of Nova Friburgo, Bishop Edney Gouvêa Mattoso, and canonical commentators state that a new latae sententiae excommunication (can. 1382) was thereby incurred; no Holy See statement on the individuals has been located.

events.title: Second Resistance consecration in Brazil
events.text: Williamson consecrates Tomás de Aquino Ferreira da Costa, prior of the Santa Cruz Monastery, without mandate.

events.title: Zendejas consecrated for the Resistance
events.text: Williamson, assisted by Faure and Tomás de Aquino, consecrates Gerardo Zendejas; the local bishop's statement notes the excommunication incurred.

events.title: Later Resistance consecrations
events.text: Williamson consecrates Paul Morgan in secret at Cork (14 February 2022), with Giacomo Ballini — whose own private 2021 consecration Williamson announced only in December 2022 — as co-consecrator, and Michał Stobnicki in Poland (15 August 2022), bringing to six the bishops he consecrated for the 'Resistance' between 2015 and 2022.

events.title: Confession faculties for the Jubilee
events.text: Pope Francis grants that the faithful can validly and licitly receive absolution from SSPX priests during the Year of Mercy.

events.title: Faculties extended indefinitely
events.text: In 'Misericordia et misera' §12 Francis extends the SSPX confession faculty 'until further provisions are made'.

events.title: Casa Autônoma do Brasil
events.text: Brazil is erected as an Autonomous House ('district in formation') of the SSPX, separated from the Argentina-based District of South America for linguistic and cultural reasons.

events.title: Marriage provisions
events.text: An Ecclesia Dei Commission letter (signed Cardinal Müller) allows local ordinaries to grant faculties so that marriages in the SSPX milieu are celebrated validly.

events.title: Pagliarani elected Superior General
events.text: The General Chapter elects Fr. Davide Pagliarani, former rector of La Reja, to a 12-year term, succeeding Bishop Fellay.

events.title: 'Traditionis Custodes'
events.text: Francis restricts the 1962 Missal in the mainstream Church. Canonically the motu proprio does not bind the SSPX; press analyses reporting increased attendance at SSPX chapels afterwards are attributed to those analyses.

events.title: Death of Tissier de Mallerais
events.text: Bishop Bernard Tissier de Mallerais, one of the four 1988 bishops and Lefebvre's biographer, dies in hospital at Martigny (Valais, Switzerland) after a fall at Écône reported in SSPX communiqués; his funeral is celebrated at the Écône seminary on 18 October 2024 and he is buried in its crypt.

events.title: Death of Richard Williamson
events.text: The expelled 1988 bishop and 'Resistance' leader dies at 84.

events.title: Election of Leo XIV
events.text: After the death of Francis (21 April 2025), Robert Prevost is elected pope; his pontificate presides over the 2026 rupture with the SSPX.

events.title: Jubilee-year pilgrimage
events.text: The SSPX holds a worldwide pilgrimage to Rome for the 2025 Jubilee.

events.title: New consecrations announced
events.text: Superior General Pagliarani announces plans to consecrate new bishops on 1 July 2026, citing the age of the Society's remaining bishops and the unresolved doctrinal disagreement.

events.title: DDF warning
events.text: Cardinal Víctor Manuel Fernández formally warns that the planned consecrations would constitute 'a schismatic act' entailing excommunication; Pope Leo XIV follows with personal appeals in June.

events.title: The 2026 Écône consecrations
events.text: Bishops de Galarreta and Fellay consecrate four new bishops — Pascal Schreiber, Michael Goldade, Michel Poinsinet de Sivry and Marc Hanappier — without papal mandate.

events.title: New excommunications declared
events.text: A DDF decree declares the six bishops involved excommunicated latae sententiae, describes SSPX clergy as being in schism, and revokes the 2015–2017 confession and marriage provisions; the SSPX rejects the decree as 'objectively unjust and invalid'. Press summaries differ on how the decree treats priests and laity — each reading is attributed.

events.title: SSPX files canonical recourse
events.text: A General House communiqué dated 13 July announces that on 11 July the Society submitted a preliminary recourse to the DDF against the 2 July decree, per cann. 1734 ff. of the Code of Canon Law; the SSPX states that the recourse suspends the decree's execution under can. 1353 — the Society's own characterization, with no Holy See response reported as of 19 July 2026.

figures.name: Marcel Lefebvre
figures.role: Founder and first Superior General (1970–1982); former Archbishop of Dakar and Superior General of the Holy Ghost Fathers; [[suspension-a-divinis|suspended a divinis]] 1976; consecrated four bishops in 1988 and was declared excommunicated; buried at Écône.

figures.name: Antônio de Castro Mayer
figures.role: Bishop of Campos, Brazil (coadjutor bishop from 1948, diocesan bishop 1949–1981), the 'other bishop of 1988': co-consecrator of the Écône consecrations. His priests formed the Priestly Union of Saint John Mary Vianney (1988), root of the Campos line that later reconciled with Rome (2002).

figures.name: Licínio Rangel
figures.role: Successor of Castro Mayer at the head of the Campos priests' union; consecrated without mandate by three SSPX bishops at São Fidélis (1991); led the union into full communion with Rome as apostolic administrator (January 2002).

figures.name: Fernando Arêas Rifan
figures.role: Apostolic administrator of the Campos administration since December 2002; appointed coadjutor by John Paul II and consecrated in full communion (2002).

figures.name: Franz Schmidberger
figures.role: Superior General 1982–1994; later German district superior and seminary rector.

figures.name: Bernard Fellay
figures.role: One of the four 1988 bishops; Superior General 1994–2018; led the 2000s–2010s negotiations with Rome; elected General Councillor on 20 July 2018 (the 2018 Chapter elected de Galarreta First Assistant, not Fellay); co-consecrator of the 2026 consecrations.

figures.name: Bernard Tissier de Mallerais
figures.role: One of the four 1988 bishops; Lefebvre's biographer; principal consecrator of Licínio Rangel for the Campos priests (São Fidélis, 1991); died at Martigny, Switzerland, after a fall, and is buried in the Écône seminary crypt.

figures.name: Richard Williamson
figures.role: One of the four 1988 bishops; expelled from the SSPX in 2012 after public defiance; led the 'Resistance', for which he consecrated six bishops without mandate (2015–2022); his 2009 Holocaust denial caused a worldwide scandal.

figures.name: Alfonso de Galarreta
figures.role: One of the four 1988 bishops; principal consecrator of the 2026 Écône consecrations.

figures.name: Davide Pagliarani
figures.role: Superior General since 2018 (12-year term); former Italy district superior and rector of La Reja; announced and defended the 2026 consecrations.

figures.name: François Charrière
figures.role: Bishop of Lausanne, Geneva and Fribourg who canonically erected the SSPX in 1970.

figures.name: Pierre Mamie
figures.role: Charrière's successor, who withdrew the Society's canonical approval in 1975.

figures.name: Paul VI
figures.role: Pope 1963–1978; publicly rebuked Lefebvre in 1976 and approved the suspension a divinis.

figures.name: John Paul II
figures.role: Pope 1978–2005; declared the 1988 consecrations a schismatic act ('Ecclesia Dei') and created the Ecclesia Dei Commission.

figures.name: Joseph Ratzinger / Benedict XVI
figures.role: As CDF prefect, negotiated the 1988 protocol; as pope (2005–2013), issued 'Summorum Pontificum' (2007) and remitted the 1988 excommunications (2009).

figures.name: Francis
figures.role: Pope 2013–2025; granted the SSPX confession faculties (2015/2016) and marriage provisions (2017) while restricting the old rite in the mainstream Church ('Traditionis Custodes', 2021).

figures.name: Leo XIV (Robert Prevost)
figures.role: Pope from May 2025; appealed to the SSPX to desist from the 2026 consecrations, calling them 'a sin of extreme gravity' in his final appeal.

figures.name: Pascal Schreiber, Michael Goldade, Michel Poinsinet de Sivry, Marc Hanappier
figures.role: The four bishops consecrated at Écône on 1 July 2026 (rectors of the Zaitzkofen and Dillwyn seminaries, the Benelux district superior, and a seminary professor respectively); declared excommunicated by the DDF on 2 July 2026.

organizations.name: FSSP — Priestly Fraternity of Saint Peter
organizations.relation: Founded on 18 July 1988 by twelve former SSPX priests who accepted 'Ecclesia Dei' after the Écône consecrations; in full communion with Rome; seminaries at Wigratzbad (Germany) and Denton, Nebraska.

organizations.name: Institute of the Good Shepherd (IBP)
organizations.relation: Society of apostolic life founded by former SSPX priests (Philippe Laguérie and others) in full communion with Rome, with statutes approved through the Ecclesia Dei Commission.

organizations.name: Society of Saint Pius V (SSPV)
organizations.relation: Founded by nine priests expelled from or leaving the SSPX in a dispute over the liturgy and sedevacantism; sedevacantist-leaning and organizationally separate from the SSPX.

organizations.name: CMRI — Congregation of Mary Immaculate Queen
organizations.relation: An explicitly sedevacantist congregation founded before the SSPX existed and never part of it — a common confusion this project flags rather than repeats.

organizations.name: The 'Resistance' (Williamson line)
organizations.relation: A loose movement — not a single institute — around Bishop Williamson after his 2012 expulsion, rejecting any accord with Rome; Williamson consecrated six bishops without mandate (2015–2022), beginning with Jean-Michel Faure at Nova Friburgo, Brazil (2015).

organizations.name: Priestly Union of Saint John Mary Vianney → Apostolic Administration (Campos)
organizations.relation: Formed on 15 August 1988 by the Campos priests loyal to Bishop Castro Mayer, the co-consecrator of the Écône consecrations; received its own bishop (Licínio Rangel, 1991) from SSPX hands; reconciled with Rome in January 2002 as the Personal Apostolic Administration Saint John Mary Vianney under Rangel and then Fernando Rifan — the 'other path' of Brazilian traditionalism.

organizations.name: Casa Autônoma do Brasil
organizations.relation: The SSPX's Brazilian autonomous house ('district in formation'), erected in 2017 from the Argentina-based District of South America; priories reported in São Paulo and other states, with formation at La Reja, Argentina.
organizations.notes: Exact founding dates of individual Brazilian priories are not yet verified — see GitHub issues.

episcopalLineage.note: Who consecrated whom. Episcopal genealogy is the clearest way to keep the traditionalist groups distinct: the SSPX and 'Resistance' bishops all descend from Marcel Lefebvre, while the Palmar de Troya (Palmarian) bishops descend from Archbishop Ngô Đình Thục and have no lineage connection to Lefebvre or the SSPX — the two are often confused. Solid connectors are direct consecrations (by the principal consecrator); dashed connectors mark documented indirect links — co-consecration, organizational succession, or a personnel association — each labeled on the edge and cited. Validity/liceity characterizations are attributed: the Holy See holds these consecrations valid but illicit (gravely unlawful), except the Palmarian line after 1978, which it treats as outside the Church entirely.

edgeLegend.indirect: Indirect link — co-consecration, succession or association (labeled on the edge)

trees.title: SSPX line — from Marcel Lefebvre
trees.summary: The 1988 Écône consecrations (co-consecrator: Antônio de Castro Mayer, emeritus of Campos, Brazil) and the 2026 consecrations performed by two of the 1988 bishops.

root.name: Marcel Lefebvre
root.detail: consecrated bishop 1947; Archbishop of Dakar; founder of the SSPX

children.name: Bernard Fellay
children.detail: Écône, 30 June 1988
children.status: 1988 excommunication declared, remitted 2009; new excommunication declared 2026 as co-consecrator (SSPX disputes it)

children.name: Bernard Tissier de Mallerais
children.detail: Écône, 30 June 1988; † 2024
children.status: 1988 excommunication declared, remitted 2009

children.name: Richard Williamson
children.detail: Écône, 30 June 1988; expelled from the SSPX 2012 — see the 'Resistance' branch; † 2025
children.status: 1988 excommunication declared, remitted 2009; new excommunications declared for his later consecrations

children.name: Alfonso de Galarreta
children.detail: Écône, 30 June 1988; principal consecrator of the 2026 consecrations (co-consecrator: Fellay)
children.status: 1988 excommunication declared, remitted 2009; new excommunication declared 2026 (SSPX disputes it)

children.name: Pascal Schreiber
children.detail: Écône, 1 July 2026; rector of the Zaitzkofen seminary
children.status: excommunication declared by the DDF, 2 July 2026 (SSPX disputes it)

children.name: Michael Goldade
children.detail: Écône, 1 July 2026; rector of the Dillwyn seminary
children.status: excommunication declared by the DDF, 2 July 2026 (SSPX disputes it)

children.name: Michel Poinsinet de Sivry
children.detail: Écône, 1 July 2026; Benelux district superior
children.status: excommunication declared by the DDF, 2 July 2026 (SSPX disputes it)

children.name: Marc Hanappier
children.detail: Écône, 1 July 2026; seminary professor
children.status: excommunication declared by the DDF, 2 July 2026 (SSPX disputes it)

trees.title: Campos line — from Antônio de Castro Mayer (Brazil)
trees.summary: The 'other bishop of 1988': Castro Mayer, emeritus of Campos (RJ), co-consecrated the Écône four. His priests had formed the Priestly Union of Saint John Mary Vianney (15 August 1988); after his death they received their own bishop from SSPX hands, and in 2002 the union reconciled with Rome as the Personal Apostolic Administration Saint John Mary Vianney — the 'other path' of Brazilian traditionalism, often contrasted with the SSPX's.

root.name: Antônio de Castro Mayer
root.detail: coadjutor bishop of Campos from 1948, Bishop of Campos 1949–1981; co-consecrator at Écône, 30 June 1988; † 25 April 1991
root.status: 1988 excommunication declared (the 2009 remission concerned the four living bishops)

children.name: The 1988 Écône four — Fellay, Tissier de Mallerais, Williamson, de Galarreta
children.detail: Castro Mayer assisted as co-consecrator at their consecration; the principal consecrator was Lefebvre — see the SSPX line

children.name: Licínio Rangel
children.edgeLabel: succession — consecrated by Tissier de Mallerais (SSPX line)
children.detail: São Fidélis (Brazil), 28 July 1991 — consecrated by Tissier de Mallerais, assisted by de Galarreta and Williamson, to lead the Campos priests' union after Castro Mayer's death; † 2002
children.status: excommunication latae sententiae per the Holy See; reconciled January 2002 as apostolic administrator

children.name: Fernando Arêas Rifan
children.detail: 2002 — appointed coadjutor by John Paul II (28 June 2002) and consecrated in full communion; succeeded Rangel in December 2002
children.status: in full communion with Rome

trees.title: 'Resistance' line — from Richard Williamson (ex-SSPX)
trees.summary: After his 2012 expulsion, Williamson consecrated six bishops for the loose 'Resistance' network (2015–2022) — two of them in Brazil. For the 2015 and 2017 consecrations, the local ordinaries (Nova Friburgo 2015; Arlington 2017) and canonical commentators stated that a new latae sententiae excommunication (can. 1382) was incurred; no Holy See statement on these individuals has been located, and none has been located for the 2016 or 2021–2022 consecrations either.

root.name: Richard Williamson
root.detail: consecrated 1988 by Lefebvre (see SSPX line); expelled from the SSPX 2012; † 2025

children.name: Jean-Michel Faure
children.detail: Santa Cruz Monastery, Nova Friburgo (Brazil), 19 March 2015
children.status: latae sententiae excommunication (can. 1382) stated by the local ordinary of Nova Friburgo, Bp. Edney Gouvêa Mattoso, and canonical commentators; no Holy See statement located

children.name: Tomás de Aquino Ferreira da Costa
children.detail: Nova Friburgo (Brazil), 19 March 2016; prior of the Santa Cruz Monastery
children.status: commentators hold a latae sententiae excommunication (can. 1382) was incurred; no individual Holy See or ordinary's statement located as of July 2026

children.name: Gerardo Zendejas
children.detail: Diocese of Arlington, Virginia (USA), 11 May 2017; co-consecrators: Faure and Tomás de Aquino
children.status: latae sententiae excommunication stated by Bp. Michael Burbidge of Arlington (8 May 2017); no Holy See statement located

children.name: Giacomo Ballini
children.detail: consecrated privately in 2021 (announced by Williamson in December 2022); leader of the Resistance's Cork branch
children.status: no individual Holy See statement located as of July 2026

children.name: Paul Morgan
children.detail: Cork (Ireland), 14 February 2022, in secret; co-consecrator: Ballini
children.status: no individual Holy See statement located as of July 2026

children.name: Michał Stobnicki
children.detail: Poland, 15 August 2022
children.status: no individual Holy See statement located as of July 2026

trees.title: Palmar de Troya line — from Ngô Đình Thục (NOT SSPX)
trees.summary: Included for disambiguation only: the Palmarian bishops of El Palmar de Troya (Seville, Spain) derive their orders from Archbishop Ngô Đình Thục, not from Lefebvre. There is no episcopal-lineage or organizational connection to the SSPX — but one documented personnel link existed: Fr. Maurice Revaz, canon of the Grand-Saint-Bernard and canon-law professor at Écône, brought Thục to Palmar in December 1975 and was himself consecrated a Palmarian bishop on 28 January 1976 by Clemente Domínguez and others — placing him in the Palmarian, not the Thục, lineage; he left the movement about two years later (Lundberg). Thục's later consecrations also seeded sedevacantist lineages (distinct again from both the SSPX and the Palmarians).

root.name: Ngô Đình Thục
root.detail: Archbishop emeritus of Huế (Vietnam), consecrated 1938; performed the Palmar consecrations in 1976

children.name: Clemente Domínguez y Gómez
children.detail: El Palmar de Troya, 11 January 1976; proclaimed himself 'Pope Gregory XVII' in 1978, founding the Palmarian Christian Church
children.status: excommunicated January 1976; the Palmarian Church is a separate body not in communion with Rome

children.name: Manuel Alonso Corral
children.detail: El Palmar de Troya, 11 January 1976; later Palmarian 'Pope Peter II' (2005–2011)
children.status: excommunicated January 1976

children.name: Three further Palmar bishops
children.detail: El Palmar de Troya, 11 January 1976
children.status: excommunicated January 1976

children.name: Maurice Revaz
children.edgeLabel: Écône personnel link — not a Thục consecration
children.detail: canon of the Grand-Saint-Bernard and canon-law professor at Écône, who drove Thục to El Palmar de Troya (December 1975); consecrated a Palmarian bishop on 28 January 1976 by Clemente Domínguez and others — placing him in the Palmarian, not the Thục, lineage; he left the movement about two years later (Lundberg, pp. 72–80)
children.status: the one documented Écône–Palmar personnel link; Lefebvre took no part in the Palmar consecrations

children.name: M.-L. Guérard des Lauriers; Moisés Carmona and Adolfo Zamora
children.detail: 1981 — Thục's later consecrations, sources of today's sedevacantist episcopal lineages (including the line behind CMRI clergy). Guérard des Lauriers had been professor of theology at Écône from 1971 until his dismissal in autumn 1977 over the vacant-see thesis — SSPX and sedeprivationist (IMBC) accounts agree — a second documented Écône personnel link to the Thục world alongside Revaz
children.status: excommunications declared; lineages distinct from both SSPX and Palmar

branchTimeline.heading: Divisions timeline
branchTimeline.note: How the groups around the SSPX forked over time, at a glance. Each fork is summarized with citations below the figure; contested characterizations are attributed there. One lane is not a split in the strict sense: the Campos priests' union was an organizationally separate Brazilian ally of the SSPX, and its lane ends where it was regularized by Rome (2002); the 2026 lane marks the rupture between the SSPX and Rome (a 'schism' per the DDF decree, disputed by the SSPX), not the departure of a group from the Society.

trunk.label: SSPX
trunk.note: founded 1 November 1970 at Fribourg by Archbishop Marcel Lefebvre; canonical approval withdrawn in 1975 — a withdrawal the SSPX disputes.

branches.label: SSPV
branches.note: Fr. Clarence Kelly and eight other US priests, expelled from or leaving the SSPX in a dispute over the 1962 Missal and openness to sedevacantism, found the Society of Saint Pius V — sedevacantist-leaning and organizationally separate.

branches.label: FSSP
branches.note: after the Écône consecrations, twelve former SSPX priests led by Fr. Josef Bisig found the Priestly Fraternity of Saint Peter, in full communion with Rome.

branches.label: Campos union
branches.note: the priests loyal to Bp. Antônio de Castro Mayer — co-consecrator of the 1988 Écône consecrations — organize the Priestly Union of Saint John Mary Vianney (15 August 1988), an SSPX ally rather than a subdivision; the lane ends in January 2002, when the union is regularized as the Personal Apostolic Administration Saint John Mary Vianney — a path the SSPX declined.

branches.label: Resistance
branches.note: Bishop Richard Williamson, expelled from the SSPX in October 2012, leads the loose 'Resistance' network, for which he consecrated six bishops without mandate (2015–2022).

branches.label: Rupture
branches.note: 1 July 2026: de Galarreta and Fellay consecrate four bishops without papal mandate. 2 July: a DDF decree declares the six bishops excommunicated and describes SSPX clergy as in schism — a decree the SSPX rejects as 'objectively unjust and invalid' and against which it filed canonical recourse on 11 July 2026.

disambiguation.note: The Society's status and identity are contested terrain. The entries below record distinctions that are easy to flatten; each characterization is attributed to its source.

items.title: SSPX is not sedevacantist
items.text: The Society has always recognized the reigning popes as legitimate, praying for them in the canon of the Mass, while resisting parts of Vatican II. [[sedevacantism|Sedevacantists]] (SSPV milieu, CMRI) hold the papal see vacant; the SSPX expelled sedevacantist-leaning priests in 1983.

items.title: SSPX ≠ FSSP
items.text: Both serve the traditional Latin Mass, but the FSSP was founded in 1988 by priests who left the SSPX precisely to remain in full communion with Rome, and has always been canonically regular.

items.title: SSPV ≠ CMRI
items.text: Both are US sedevacantist-milieu groups, but they are unrelated organizations; only the SSPV is an SSPX splinter — the CMRI predates the SSPX and never belonged to it.

items.title: Canonical status must be dated
items.text: 1970–1975: approved ([[pia-unio|pia unio]]). 1975–2026: [[canonical-irregularity|canonically irregular]] — 'no canonical status' in Benedict XVI's 2009 wording — but repeatedly described by Vatican officials as not formally [[schism|schismatic]], with confession and marriage provisions granted 2015–2017. Since 2 July 2026: a [[cdf-ddf|DDF]] decree declares the consecrations schismatic, the six bishops excommunicated and the clergy in schism, and revokes those provisions; the SSPX disputes the decree's validity, and on 11 July 2026 filed a preliminary canonical recourse against it (cann. 1734 ff.), claiming suspensive effect under can. 1353 — the Society's own claim, with no Holy See response reported as of 19 July 2026. Each characterization is attributed to its author.

items.title: SSPX ≠ Palmar de Troya
items.text: The Palmarian Church of El Palmar de Troya (Spain) is sometimes confused with the SSPX. Its bishops were consecrated in 1976 by Archbishop Ngô Đình Thục — not by Lefebvre — and from 1978 it elected its own popes, placing it outside the Catholic Church entirely; the SSPX has always recognized the Roman pontiff. The one documented personnel connection is Fr. Maurice Revaz, canon-law professor at Écône, who brought Thục to Palmar (December 1975) and became a Palmarian bishop (28 January 1976), leaving about two years later (Lundberg). Lefebvre repudiated the comparison in his 30 June 1988 consecration sermon: there is no question 'of establishing a sort of parallel church as the Bishops of Palmar de Troya have done in Spain… It is out of the question for us to do such things.' See the episcopal genealogy section.

items.title: Names and domains
items.text: Portuguese: Fraternidade Sacerdotal São Pio X (FSSPX); English: Society of Saint Pius X (SSPX); Latin: Fraternitas Sacerdotalis Sancti Pii X. fsspx.org is the General House, sspx.org the US district, fsspx.news the news organ, fsspx.com.br the Brazilian house.
